source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) is in use

input_row.source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
input_row.title: Ensure AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) is in use
input_row.description: AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) lets you securely control your users' access to AWS services and resources. To manage access control for Amazon DynamoDB, you can create IAM policies that control access to tables and data.
input_row.rationale: IAM policies help you control and maintain access to Amazon DynamoDB as needed.
input_row.audit: 1. Open IAM Console • Sign in to the AWS Management Console and open the IAM console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/. 2. Navigate to Policies • In the IAM console, in the navigation pane, choose Policies. 3. Create Policy • Choose Create policy. • You will be taken to the Create policy page. 4. Choose Service • Click on Choose a service. • Type DynamoDB in the search box and select it. 5. Configure Actions • Under the Actions section, select the actions you want to allow the user to perform. • For instance, you can select Read to allow read actions like GetItem, Scan, Query, etc.   6. Set Resources • Under the Resources section, you can specify which tables this policy applies to. • You can choose "All resources" or specify the ARN (Amazon Resource Name) of specific tables. 7. Review Policy • Click on Review policy. • Give your policy a name and description. • Then click Create policy. • Now, you have an IAM policy. 8. Attach Policy • Navigate to the Users, Groups, or Roles section in the IAM console. • Choose an existing user, group, or role, or create a new one. • Once you've selected a user, group, or role, click Add permissions. • Choose Attach existing policies directly. • Search for your created policy, select it, and click Attach policy. • With these steps, you have attached an IAM policy that controls access to DynamoDB resources.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control is about ensuring AWS IAM is used to manage access to DynamoDB. The audit steps provided describe creating and attaching IAM policies that reference DynamoDB, but there is no API-exposed property or flag at the table or account level that confirms whether IAM is being used in practice to restrict or allow DynamoDB access. While you can use AWS APIs to list IAM policies and their attachments, confirming that every DynamoDB access is exclusively and properly governed by IAM (and not legacy resource policies or non-standard mechanisms) requires manual verification. It involves reviewing policy contents, their application to users/roles/groups, and comprehension of intent—this is not readily determinable by automation alone.

Though AWS APIs (like list_policies, get_policy, list_entities_for_policy) can enumerate IAM policies referencing DynamoDB actions or resources, automated tools cannot fully determine:
- Whether these policies are actually enforced across all DynamoDB access scenarios,
- If no direct/legacy access is being used,
- The appropriateness or principle-of-least-privilege of the configured policies,
without human interpretation.

Therefore, this is a **Manual** check.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Open the IAM Console:**
   - Sign in to the AWS Management Console and navigate to the IAM console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/.

2. **Review IAM Policies:**
   - In the IAM console navigation pane, click on "Policies."
   - Search for existing IAM policies that reference DynamoDB by filtering for service: “dynamodb”.

3. **Policy Content Verification:**
   - For each relevant policy, click on its name to review the JSON policy document.
   - Confirm the policy includes DynamoDB actions (such as “dynamodb:GetItem”, “dynamodb:PutItem”, etc.) and resource ARNs that correspond to your DynamoDB tables.

4. **Check Attachments:**
   - Navigate to "Users," "Groups," and "Roles" sections.
   - For each principal allowed to access DynamoDB, verify which IAM policies are attached and whether they correspond to those discovered in Step 2.

5. **Ensure IAM Use for DynamoDB Access:**
   - Verify that access to all DynamoDB tables is managed via IAM policies.
   - Ensure no access is granted through deprecated means or ungoverned resource policies.

6. **Verify Principle of Least Privilege:**
   - Confirm that only necessary actions are granted and that access is restricted to only required DynamoDB tables.

7. **Document Compliance:**
   - Record which principals have DynamoDB access and detail the policies that grant such access.

**Expected Secure Configuration:**
- All access to DynamoDB resources is strictly controlled through appropriately scoped IAM policies attached to identities (users, groups, or roles).
- No user or service has ungoverned access to DynamoDB tables.
- Principle of least privilege is enforced.

**SOURCE:** CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
